region_of_origin: Taiwan (popularised across Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for milk-free bubble tea; A0FKP (tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bubble tea beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of bubble tea (no milk) is approximately 500 g, comprising brewed tea, sugar syrup, ice, and tapioca pearls.

nutrient_source.title: Bubble Tea (No Milk) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo database entry for bubble tea without milk (cr_id D08010003) used as primary nutrient source. Sodium value reported as -1 in source, indicating data not available in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Tapioca (boiled in salted water) and sugar-sweetened tea beverage — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for milk-free bubble tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the composite of: (1) boiled tapioca pearls (GI ~70–94, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) and (2) sugar-sweetened tea (GI ~65–70). A weighted composite yields GI ~70 (high). Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 36.75) / 100 = 25.7. Input record GI value of 70 and GL of 5.1 (per 100 g) are consistent with this estimate; GL per serving recalculated for a 500 g cup.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar syrup with negligible fibre and potassium; sodium data unavailable but beverage is not a significant sodium source
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid sugar-sweetened bubble tea because AHA guidelines identify sugar-sweetened beverages as independently associated with elevated blood pressure; choose genuinely unsweetened black or green tea with no syrup and no tapioca pearls.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar syrup and high-GI tapioca pearls (GI 70); rapid postprandial glucose spike
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bubble tea without milk is a sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar syrup and high-GI tapioca pearls; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this drink entirely and substitute with unsweetened black or green tea to prevent postprandial hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content from syrup; empty-calorie beverage with no fibre, no beneficial fats, and no micronutrient density relevant to cardiovascular protection
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened bubble tea contributes to excess free sugar intake, which is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate sugar-sweetened beverages per WHO and AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar syrup and refined tapioca starch promote pro-inflammatory mediators; no net anti-inflammatory nutritional value
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bubble tea without milk contains added sugar syrup and refined tapioca pearls that promote inflammatory cytokine production; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace this beverage with unsweetened green tea to obtain polyphenol anti-inflammatory benefit without the sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubble Tea (No Milk) is a popular Taiwanese-origin beverage widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It consists of brewed tea — typically black, green, or fruit-infused — sweetened with sugar syrup and served with chewy tapioca pearls (boba) over ice, without any milk or dairy component. The beverage has a sweet, lightly astringent tea flavour with a characteristic chewy texture from the tapioca pearls. It is sold in a wide range of sweetness levels and can be customised with different toppings. Nutritionally, bubble tea without milk is a low-protein, zero-fat beverage with moderate carbohydrate content (7.35 g/100 g) derived primarily from added sugar syrup (4.45 g sugar/100 g) and tapioca starch. Energy density is low at 29 kcal/100 g, but a standard 500 g serving delivers approximately 145 kcal and 36.75 g of carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high), driven by the high-GI tapioca pearls and sugar syrup, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 25.7 per 500 g serving. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its added sugar content, high glycemic index, negligible fibre (0.03 g/100 g), and absence of protective micronutrients. It is dairy-free, vegan, and gluten-free, making it suitable for those with lactose intolerance or dairy allergies, but the sugar content remains a concern across all metabolic health conditions.
common_user_queries: Is bubble tea without milk lower in calories?
What is the sugar content of bubble tea?
Is bubble tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of bubble tea?
How many calories in a cup of bubble tea?
Can I drink bubble tea if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010003), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous foods (boiled tapioca and sugar-sweetened tea) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry D08010003; bubble tea without milk contains no protein-contributing ingredients (no dairy, no soy, no nuts). fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry D08010003; the beverage contains no fat-contributing ingredients (no milk, no cream, no oil). saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are set to 0.0 as confirmed zero by formulation (dairy-free, fat-free beverage). sodium_mg is set to -1: the HPB Foodo source reports sodium as -1 (data not available) for this entry; sodium data for this specific preparation is not available in the HPB database as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this specific bubble tea preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled tapioca pearls and sugar-sweetened tea as reference foods; no direct GI entry exists for milk-free bubble tea. Glycemic load per serving recalculated for a 500 g standard takeaway cup: GL = (70 × 36.75) / 100 = 25.7. The input record GL of 5.1 appears to be per 100 g basis, which is consistent. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 used as closest defensible match for bubble tea; FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as adapted parent category for tea-based beverages.
